edges.description: Abnormal glycogen initiation leads to polyglucosan body accumulation in skeletal muscle fibers.
edges.source: GYG1 deficiency and failed glycogen synthesis initiation

edges.description: Polyglucosan bodies in glycogenin-1-deficient muscle sequester proteins involved in glycogen metabolism, protein quality control, p62, and desmin.
edges.source: GYG1 deficiency and failed glycogen synthesis initiation

edges.description: A subset of patients develop cardiac involvement with polyglucosan storage.
edges.source: GYG1 deficiency and failed glycogen synthesis initiation

edges.description: Proteostasis burden and normal-glycogen depletion within affected fibers add a myofiber dysfunction branch to the storage myopathy.
intermediate_mechanisms: depletion of normal glycogen in affected fibers

edges.description: Affected fibers can show depletion of normal glycogen despite polyglucosan storage.
Limb-girdle myopathy manifests as proximal weakness of pelvic and shoulder girdle muscles.
Early muscle dysfunction produces reduced exercise tolerance and exertional symptoms.
Some patients present with predominant distal weakness or mixed proximal-distal involvement.
Muscle fiber dysfunction produces exertional and chronic myalgia.
Pelvic-girdle weakness can produce a waddling gait.
Shoulder-girdle weakness can produce scapular winging.
Progressive polyglucosan myopathy is reflected by skeletal-muscle atrophy and fatty replacement on whole-body muscle MRI.
Skeletal-muscle polyglucosan myopathy can be reflected by elevated serum creatine kinase.
intermediate_mechanisms: skeletal myofiber injury and enzyme leakage into serum

edges.description: Cardiac involvement can manifest as rhythm disturbance.
Elevated serum creatine kinase reports skeletal myofiber injury in the GYG1-related polyglucosan myopathy branch.
Depletion of normal glycogen in fibers containing polyglucosan bodies reports the abnormal glycogen/protein storage compartment.

nodes.description: Polyglucosan bodies in glycogenin-1-deficient skeletal muscle are not only glycogen-rich inclusions; they accumulate proteins from glycogen metabolism, protein quality control pathways, sequestosome 1/p62, and desmin, and affected fibers can show depletion of normal glycogen.
